next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

The brace was off. Petra had said short stretches were fine as long as he did not get stupid with it, which left Kai the problem of deciding what counted as stupid. The graft-driven repair had run six weeks too fast. The bone in his left wrist no longer felt broken. It felt crowded. Something busy and needling worked at the join, new tissue packing itself tight around old fracture lines and synthetic anchors, his body hurrying through a timetable he had not agreed to.

Kai sat on the edge of his bed and put his left hand flat on the mattress.

He had started three nights ago, after his hand went early for a glass of water and he caught the quick flutter in the rebuilt forearm. Since then he had been trying to catch that flutter before the motion finished, before the hand got to pretend it had been his idea all along.

Index finger. He told himself to lift it, then waited.

There it was. A low gather in the synthetic muscle, too small for anyone else to see. He pressed the finger harder into the mattress and held. One beat. Two. Three. The pressure thickened, pushed up his forearm, found the welded ache in the wrist. Sweat pricked under his T-shirt even though the room was cold.

When he let go, the finger lifted at once.

Not much. Barely anything. Enough.

He tried again with the middle finger and got nothing but pain. Third try, the same small early kick. He caught it for half a beat, maybe less, and the success sent an ugly little flare of satisfaction through him. Private override training, one finger at a time, in a room that smelled like radiator heat and...
open_hooks_to_carry: Two more athletes report sensory anomalies—hearing loss, hand numbness
Yusuf confirms virus spreading through shared calibration protocols
Lena visits Kai in medical—their shared condition creates a bond
Margot realizes the neural coaching feed may be a transmission vector and does not report it
prev_tail_excerpt:
She turned back.

"Am I the worst one so far?"

The question put the first hesitation into her body all morning. It wasn't much. A fractional stop before the answer.

"You are the worst one we know about so far," she said.

Then she went out.

The room got quiet again, but it wasn't the same quiet as before. This one had other rooms inside it. Doors were closing across the facility. Training was being cancelled mid-rep. Athletes were walking back to quarters with their bodies still attached to a network they had just been told not to trust. Somewhere down the hall, Yusuf was running toward the calibration bay before the logs disappeared into summary.

Kai looked down the length of the exam table. The sheet over his legs rose in two low ridges. Both feet pointed where they were supposed to point.

They stayed still.

He watched long enough for stillness to stop meaning safety and start meaning only this second, and this second, and this second after that.

The call button sat clipped to the rail by his good hand. It was plastic, off-white, ordinary.

He pressed it.

When the speaker answered, he said, "Could someone stay in here a bit?"

The reply came at once. "Yes. Someone's coming."

Footsteps passed in the corridor. More footsteps followed. A cart rattled by. A door slammed somewhere down the hall. The building had not settled yet and wasn't going to.

Kai kept his eyes on his feet and waited for the next movement to be his.

setups_to_plant.description: Margot's direct neural feed connection may be an additional vector for virus propagation between athletes

state_deltas_required: The virus is systemic; every enhanced athlete at the facility is potentially compromised
